text: Anytime someone gives me the opportunity to be Guy Voller, I'm going to take that opportunity. It's just you can't pass that up. I'm going to talk about peristomal hernia repair. In the middle of this, I actually have Guy's slides, and so we'll go into his topic kind of in the middle of my talk here as well.

These are my disclosures, which don't have any relevance to what we're going to talk about. I don't think I have to tell this audience that peristomal hernias are common. They affect up to fifty percent of stomas. I think if you get CAT scans or look for people, you will find people with non-symptomatic peristomal hernias at a much higher percentage than that. We know that these are technically difficult to repair, that they do have a higher surgical site occurrence rate, a higher recurrence rate as well.

And you can add into this the problems of stoma-related complications such as obstruction at the stoma site, injury to the stoma site, necrosis, and kinking from stoma repairs. We also know that midline hernias are very, very commonly found with these as well. So when you put this constellation together, this is a problem that we do not have yet a very good solution to.

There's lots of options for repairing these with mesh. We do know that mesh-based repairs are the preferred way to fix these. Keyhole repair can be done as an intra-abdominal, an onlay, or as a retromuscular repair. You can sugar baker the mesh, and for a while I think sugar baker was confined to the intraperitoneal space. We're going to show you a little bit at the end how we've moved that into the retromuscular plane. You can also cruciate the mesh in any number of locations, and I'll touch on that a little bit as part of Guy's talk. My repair algorithm for peristomals is basically the following.

I reserve laparoscopic sugar baker repairs for those that are relatively small peristomal hernias or that have a smaller or minimal midline component. Once somebody has a large peristomal hernia or has a moderate or large midline hernia in combination with it, I prefer to do a posterior component separation, typically using a transversus abdominis release. Your options for doing that include moving the ostomy to a new location or cruciating the mesh or utilizing a stapled ostomy reinforcement procedure. If the ostomy can't be relocated, you can keyhole the mesh around it, but literature supports not keyholing meshes as often as possible.

And what I'm going to talk about today is sort of our newest modification of this algorithm, which is to intentionally leave the ostomy where it is presently located and retromuscularize it utilizing a PPHR technique. So how do we do a TAR with an ostomy in sight? Well, the first thing you have to realize is you've seen several videos of TARs here. When you do a TAR and there's an ostomy that you're taking down, there will be a defect in the posterior layer that's just part and parcel of the operation, and you simply sew that up. You'll then begin in the upper abdomen and release the transversus muscle. You've seen some very good videos of it done robotically. Those are the best videos you're going to see because you get a very good view. This is hard to show in an open technique.

The video shows a cross-sectional view of a surgical repair involving the relocation of an ostomy. The old stoma site on the left is reinforced with mesh, and the midline is also reinforced with mesh. The mesh is cut to allow the new ostomy to pass through, which is positioned within three or four centimeters of the midline. This configuration highlights the potential risk of compromising the midline repair due to the mesh being cut at the new ostomy site.

Mike Rosen and Yuri Novitskiy published their initial experience with this technique. Seventy percent of their patients in this series had a midline incisional hernia as well, which gave them a very large hernia area of almost two hundred and thirty centimeters in these patients. At thirteen months, they had eleven percent recurrence rate, and most of those recurrences were located at the new ostomy site. Whether you want to call that a new hernia or a recurrent hernia, it's neither here nor there. The patient had a peristomal hernia, and they have a new peristomal hernia that requires a repair. They had the anticipated amount of postoperative problems, including deep and superficial surgical site infections. They had one mucocutaneous separation. Again, these are problems inherent to managing people who have an ostomy. And I will point out that they had one patient with an ischemic stoma in that series. But I think ultimately that repair gives you a very acceptable risk of recurrence. It does relocate the ostomy. The new site is reinforced. You get a Y sublay overlap. You can repair midline defects, and I think there's pretty good support that it's safe to use polypropylene mesh in those circumstances.

However, the ostomy does need to be moved. So now you've got an old wound site and a new wound site. You've transected the bowel. You've mobilized it to a new location. It's probably a little bit more contaminated than if you simply covered the ostomy and left it in place. and the mesh is cut, and as I said earlier, most of those hernias happen where the ostomy site was located. In particular, in this case, the other problem is that mesh configuration is tricky. You're trying to make a hole in the posterior sheath in the mesh and in the new ostomy site in the native abdominal wall, and lining those three spots up with one another can be very tricky. To help make that easier, people have looked at this idea of stapled reinforcement technique or a smart technique, and these were guy slides. This was first described in two thousand eleven as a way to prophylax ostomy sites against a hernia recurrence. It's a very easy way to place the mesh.

It prevents the abdominal wall and the mesh in the new ostomy location from sliding or kinking on one another. and really prevents a lot of mesh migration toward the location where the bowel is located while the repair is healing. You really don't have to stretch the muscle because you're using a stapler to do it. And while they initially described this with a very specially designed stapler, you can simply do this with any EEA stapler. These are the pictures from their original description of the technique, where they've made a small hole in the subcutaneous layer. They use the anvil of what essentially is an EEA stapler. The meshes fired into that space in the bowel delivered through the middle of the mesh in the trophene made in the abdominal wall. There have been subsequent reports on this. In hernia in two thousand fifteen, Williams reported twenty-two patients. There were a combination of old and new ostomy sites. With a median follow-up of twenty-one months, they reported nineteen percent paralysomal hernia recurrence with the SMART technique and seventy-three percent if you just relocated. So good evidence for using this to prophylax. In a more recent study in two thousand seventeen, fourteen patients had the same technique prophylaxis for colon sites that were created. They had two recurrences with a median follow up of two years.

I personally wrote a little rebuttal to this article just sort of criticizing the technique a little bit. I kind of disagree with using a stapler to resect portions of the native abdominal wall. And while the idea that stapling the mesh to the abdominal wall helps it and theoretically prevents that size from being open, I can assure you that that is not the case. This is a patient from my institution. I have a surgeon who'd been doing this technique long before the original descriptions of it. He simply never published it. And so this is onlay polypropylene mesh, and this was a twenty-one millimeter EEA stapler site, which now measures almost four millimeters in size, four centimeters in size. and now has a peristomal hernia associated with it. So the staples, they're not interconnected. They don't prevent that ostomy site from expanding over the course of time. And so I don't know in the long term whether this here is not a good idea. Yuri Novitskiy has published utilizing a TAR technique with what he described as a STORM or a stapled transabdominal ostomy reinforcement. This is essentially the way, instead of cruciating the mesh where the new ostomy is going to be located with that TAR procedure that I showed you earlier, of rather utilizing the stapler to create that trophene.

So these are Yuri's slides. I'm giving guys talk with Yuri's slides. I kind of like this. The tar is performed in the upper abdomen, similar to the technique you've seen earlier. The bowel is brought through the posterior sheath and delivered at the site that will be planned for the new ostomy location. You then align all of the layers, and again, this is really where the sort of art of this repair comes in place. You've got to make sure that all three of those layers are lined up appropriately before you start making holes in them to deliver the bowel through. An anvil is placed through the posterior sheath and through the mesh and then is connected through the abdominal wall to the anvil. So now you've got all three layers of the abdominal wall together. The bowel, the stapler is fired, and the bowel can then be delivered through those at the new location. In their initial series, which is, I believe, in press right now, they had twelve patients with a pretty high BMI overall.

They used synthetic mesh the majority of the time. They had the anticipated amount of surgical site events for a peristomal repair, and they did have two recurrences within a year of follow-up. So on par with what they had already previously published, You can also do the same technique in an onlay. This is one of Guy's slides showing the onlay mesh which has been stapled to the abdominal wall. It is also fibrin sealed to the abdominal wall. And then you can see the trophene that has stapled reinforcement in place. The bowel is then delivered through that onlay trophene. So Guy's conclusion was that the jury is still out on this technique. Whether or not this prevents recurrences, I don't know. But if you're doing a TAR and are moving the ostomy to a new location, I think it is a very nice way to make sure that those three layers are lined up, you know, in a very precise fashion, that they don't have the opportunity to migrate or shift in the very early postoperative period. So I think for that purpose, it clearly makes sense.

The surgeon performs a retromuscular dissection, completing a transversus abdominis release while leaving the ostomy in place. This involves a retrorectus release around the stoma, demonstrating the complexity of the procedure, particularly in obese patients.

The surgeon carefully avoids injuring the linea semilunaris and the ostomy while intentionally creating a defect in the posterior sheath. The bowel is moved from a medial location in the posterior sheath to a lateral position in the preperitoneal or pre-transversalis space, and the defect is sewn up from lateral to medial.

The surgeon places the bowel in a sugar baker configuration with the peritoneum visible below it. The posterior rectus sheath is closed in the midline, creating a large space for mesh placement in the sublay space. Different mesh versions are shown, with the mesh positioned far lateral without fixation, using fibrin sealant to maintain its position.

The surgeon utilizes an intermediate-weight polypropylene mesh to perform a Sugarbaker configuration for the bowel. The mesh provides a wide overlap over the midline defect without being cut near the midline. The bowel enters the retromuscular plane on the patient's far right side and follows a path to the midline. A robotic technique is employed to take down the posterior sheath, perform a transversus abdominis release (TAR) around the ostomy, lateralize the bowel, and close the posterior layer. The mesh size is measured for overlap, and trans-fascial fixation sutures are placed on the right and left sides.

The mesh is positioned in a Sugarbaker configuration, with the robot placed on the ipsilateral side to the ostomy. The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release (TAR) on the opposite side and unrolls the mesh, addressing peristomal and midline hernias. Pre-procedure and post-procedure images show the bowel entering the retromuscular plane at a shallow angle, with no hernia recurrence observed at one-year follow-up.

The video shows a patient with a large midline defect and a small peristomal defect. The midline defect is visible before and after closure, with the stoma site lateralized and reinforced. The slide displays the patient's condition before and after the repair, highlighting the successful reinforcement of the stoma site.

You know, there's a lot of things about this patient that make it a challenging patient. It's a Crohn's patient with a multiply recurrent hernia. You know, ultimately, mesh configuration around any ostomy can result in problems, and this operation is ultimately no different than that. So I think before everybody goes home and starts doing this, you know, the jury is still out on this as well, just like a guy concluded I'm going to conclude the same thing. This may be a good idea, this may be a really terrible idea, but I'm going to leave this slide up so that we all at least recognize that it may be a problem.
Thank you very much.
